assessments.rationale: Product is certified to EN 179 and EN 1125. Hospital fire codes (NFPA 101) require identical single-motion escape standard. No gap.
Product satisfies this absolute constraint by design. Mechanical trigger operates at 0V; battery depletion disables only the IoT module.

Large multi-wing hospital buildings frequently exceed 30m between exits and gateway. Reinforced concrete and lead-lined radiology rooms attenuate 868 MHz signals. Repeater infrastructure resolves this.
assessments.mitigation: Deploy wireless repeater network (one per 30m span); Conduct RF site survey to identify attenuation zones; Install additional gateway units per floor/wing cluster

assessments.rationale: High door-event frequency in ED, ICU corridors, and psychiatric ward exits can deplete 9V battery below 2-year target. Structured maintenance schedule and monitoring alerts resolve this.
assessments.mitigation: Activate IoT dashboard battery-status alerts; Classify doors by traffic tier: quarterly vs. annual replacement; Configure low-power TX mode during overnight low-activity periods

assessments.rationale: Hospital emergency exit doors are predominantly standard fire-rated steel doors where surface mounting is accepted practice. Visible device provides active deterrent in elopement-prevention contexts.
Hospital BMS platforms operate on BACnet/IP; nurse-call systems on proprietary protocols. EasyWave has no native interoperability. GfS BUS Interface bridges to BACnet with integration development.
assessments.mitigation: Deploy GfS wireless BUS Interface as BACnet/IP bridge; Develop API connector between GfS monitoring software and hospital CMMS; Provide BMS integration schema for JCI/JCAHO audits

assessments.rationale: 100-150 door hospital deployment at ~730 EUR/door = EUR 73K-110K. Within healthcare capital budget thresholds but requires structured procurement case. Cost is never a knockout.
assessments.mitigation: Develop formal ROI model quantifying patrol labor elimination and compliance savings; Negotiate volume pricing for whole-hospital deployment contracts; Structure phased rollout starting with highest-risk zones

assessments.rationale: Door-event logs in psychiatric, oncology, and pediatric zones may constitute personal data under GDPR if attributable to identifiable patients. Both GDPR and HIPAA compliance paths are well-established.
assessments.mitigation: Implement data minimization: store event type + timestamp + door ID only; Execute DPA with cloud monitoring provider; Complete DPIA for EU sites; execute HIPAA BAA for US sites

assessments.rationale: Standard hospital areas are dry and IP45 is sufficient. Wet areas (sluice rooms, decontamination zones) require IP65+. Specifying IP45 as hospital minimum eliminates this gap for most doors.
assessments.mitigation: Specify IP45 variant as minimum for all hospital installations; Identify IP65/IP67 variant for wet-area installations; Confirm IoT module EMC immunity compliance for co-existence with medical imaging RF

assessments.rationale: Hospitals combine electronic access control with emergency exit functionality in psychiatric wards, pharmacies, and ICU. Combined configurations require EN 13637 system-level compliance.
assessments.mitigation: Define wiring specification isolating Türwächter from powered lock control paths; Require certified system integrator for EN 13637 combined-system compliance; Classify doors by type: standalone vs. combined requiring integrator sign-off
